## **Public Complaints and Review Commission Act**
**S.C. 2024, c. 25**
Assented to 2024-10-31
An Act establishing the Public Complaints and Review Commission and amending certain Acts and statutory instruments
His Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada, enacts as follows:
## **Short Title**
The following provision is not in force.
**Marginal note:Short title**
**1** This Act may be cited as the _Public Complaints and Review Commission Act_.
## **Interpretation**
The following provision is not in force.
**Marginal note:Definitions**
*   The following provision is not in force.
    
    **2** **(1)** The following definitions apply in this Act.
    
    **_Agency_** means the Canada Border Services Agency. (_Agence_)
    
    **_CBSA employee_** means any person who is or was an officer or employee of the Agency. (_employé de l’ASFC_ )
    
    **_child_** means a person who is or, in the absence of any evidence to the contrary, appears to be under the age of 18 years. (_enfant_)
    
    **_Commission_** means the Public Complaints and Review Commission established by subsection 3(1). (_Commission_)
    
    **_Commissioner_** means the Commissioner of the Royal Canadian Mounted Police. (_commissaire_)
    
    **_employee of the Agency_** includes any person who assists or assisted the Agency in the exercise of any of its powers or the performance of any of its duties and functions under the _Canada Border Services Agency Act_, other than a person who assists or assisted the Agency by reason only of an agreement or arrangement referred to in subsection 13(3) of that Act. (_employé de l’Agence_ )
    
    **_Minister_** means the Minister of Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness. (_ministre_)
    
    **_President_** means the President of the Agency. (_président_)
    
    **_proceedings_** means any investigation or hearing conducted by the Commission with respect to a complaint made under Part 2 or 3. (_procédure_)
    
    **_program legislation_** has the same meaning as in section 2 of the _Canada Border Services Agency Act_. (_législation frontalière_)
    
    **_RCMP_** means the Royal Canadian Mounted Police. (_Gendarmerie_)
    
    **_RCMP employee_** means any person who is or was
    
    *   The following provision is not in force.
        
        **(a)** a _member_, as defined in subsection 2(1) of the _Royal Canadian Mounted Police Act_; or
        
    *   The following provision is not in force.
        
        **(b)** a person appointed or employed under Part I of that Act. (_employé de la GRC_)
        
*   The following provision is not in force.
    
    **Marginal note:Designation**
    
    **(2)** Every person designated under subsection 9(2) of the _Canada Border Services Agency Act_ is an officer or employee of the Canada Border Services Agency for the purposes of this Act and, when that person is exercising any power or performing any duty or function by reason of the designation, that person is deemed to be exercising a power or performing a duty or function under the _Canada Border Services Agency Act_.
    
*   The following provision is not in force.
    
    **Marginal note:Complaints regarding level of service — RCMP**
    
    **(3)** For the purposes of subsections 33(1) and 36(1), the taking of, or the failure to take, a decision in relation to the level of any service provided by the RCMP by a person who, at the time the decision was taken, or not taken, as the case may be, was an RCMP employee is deemed to be conduct by the person in the performance of a duty or function under the _Royal Canadian Mounted Police Act_.
    
*   The following provision is not in force.
    
    **Marginal note:Provincial ministers not RCMP employees**
    
    **(4)** For greater certainty, nothing in subsection (3), 33(1) or 36(1) is to be construed as indicating that a provincial minister involved in the determination of the level of service provided by the RCMP in the province is acting as an RCMP employee.
    
*   The following provision is not in force.
    
    **Marginal note:Complaints regarding level of service — Agency**
    
    **(5)** For the purposes of subsections 33(2) and 36(2), the taking of, or the failure to take, a decision in relation to the level of any service provided by the Agency by a person who, at the time the decision was taken, or not taken, as the case may be, was an officer or employee of the Agency is deemed to be conduct by the person in the exercise of a power or the performance of a duty or function under the _Canada Border Services Agency Act_.
    
*   The following provision is not in force.
    
    **Marginal note:Deeming**
    
    **(6)** For the purposes of this Act, an employee of the Agency who assists or assisted the Agency in the exercise of any of its powers or the performance of any of its duties and functions under the _Canada Border Services Agency Act_ is deemed to be exercising or to have exercised those powers or to be performing or to have performed those duties and functions.

# 关于PCRC成立前过渡期的处理规定

## 一、过渡期条款（Transitional Provisions）

法案第113条规定了详细的过渡安排：

### 1. **投诉处理的连续性**
```
Section 113(9): 任何在旧委员会（CRCC）未处理完的投诉，
可以由新委员会（PCRC）按照新法案继续处理
```

### 2. **投诉可追溯性**
```
Section 113(10): 投诉可以针对以下时间发生的行为：
- 新法生效前发生的行为
- 生效当天发生的行为  
- 生效后发生的行为
```

## 二、当前实际情况

### 目前状态：
- **法案已通过**：2024年10月31日获得Royal Assent
- **尚未生效**：等待总督令（Order in Council）
- **Section 146明确规定**：各条款将在总督令指定的日期生效

### 过渡期投诉处理：
在PCRC正式成立前，投诉继续由现有机构处理：

1. **RCMP相关投诉** → CRCC（现有民间审查委员会）
2. **CBSA相关投诉** → 暂无独立投诉机制

## 三、关键时间点

```
2024-10-31: 法案通过
     ↓
[等待期间]
     ↓
待定日期: 总督令发布 → PCRC正式成立
     ↓
之后: 新投诉机制全面运作
```

## 四、公众投诉建议

### 当前阶段：
1. **RCMP投诉**：仍向CRCC投诉
2. **CBSA投诉**：可向CBSA内部投诉
3. **准备**：收集证据，待PCRC成立后可正式投诉

### 注意事项：
- 法案允许对"过去行为"投诉
- 但Section 33规定有**2年时效**
- 可申请延期，但需充分理由

**关键提示**：虽然PCRC未成立，但不影响投诉权利的保留，建议先通过现有渠道投诉并保存记录。

# **Regulations for the Administration of the Public Complaints and Review Commission Act**
## **Enabling Act**
_Public Complaints and Review Commission Act_
## **Description**
On October 31, 2024, an _Act establishing the Public Complaints and Review Commission_ (PCRC Act) received Royal Assent. The Act creates a dual-purpose independent review body called the Public Complaints and Review Commission (PCRC). The PCRC will replace the existing Civilian Review and Complaints Commission (CRCC) for the Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) and extend its mandate to the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA).
The objective of this regulatory initiative is to establish regulations for carrying out the purposes and provisions of this Act, and under the CBSA Act as amended through the PCRC Act, including carrying over important regulations from the CRCC model and establishing new regulations to provide further direction on the processing of complaints. This aims to enhance the accountability and transparency mechanisms and support the overall effectiveness of the PCRC.
The Act provides the Governor-in-Council regulation making powers, including for information sharing and related procedures (e.g., protection of information, procedural service standards), as well as delineation of mandates and coordination with other bodies such as the National Security and Intelligence Review Agency (NSIRA).
## **Potential impacts on Canadians, including businesses**
The regulatory initiative is expected to impact various stakeholders who have long called for independent review of Canadian law enforcement agencies or who may have concerns about increased scrutiny. The groups that will be most impacted include:
1.  RCMP and CBSA employees
    
2.  First Nations, Inuit and Métis people
    
3.  Racialized communities
    
4.  Civil Liberties and legal organizations/groups
    
5.  RCMP and CBSA employee Unions
    
There are no expected impacts on international trade or investment.
## **Regulatory cooperation efforts (domestic and international)**
Not applicable.
## **Consultations**
Public Safety Canada (PS) has consulted internally with portfolio partners including the current review body (the CRCC) as well as the NSIRA. Over the coming months, PS is anticipating consulting external stakeholders, including CBSA and RCMP employee unions as well as human rights, civil liberties, indigenous, racialized and legal groups and organizations on regulations pursuant to the Act.
## **Further information**

Dear Members of the NSICOP,
I am writing to formally bring to your attention a matter of systemic inefficiency and lack of accountability within the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB). I submit this report **on behalf of a collective of 15** (myself and 14 others, listed at the end of this email) admitted students to the University of British Columbia (UBC) to demonstrate that this is not an isolated grievance, but a widespread failure in processing protocols that warrants the Committee's urgent review.
I am fully aware that the mandate of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) is to review the legislative, regulatory, policy, administrative, and financial framework for national security and intelligence, and that the Committee does not typically investigate individual complaints.
However, we are approaching NSICOP to report a **systemic administrative failure**. We allege that the CBSA is operating in a regulatory vacuum, engaging in subjective inaction regarding security screenings for Chinese students, with no effective oversight mechanism currently in place to hold them accountable.
### Exhaustion of Other Review Mechanisms
We turn to NSICOP as a measure of last resort because all other avenues for oversight have been exhausted or are unavailable:
1.  **Internal CBSA Complaints:** They refused to accept such complaints.
    
2.  **NSIRA (National Security and Intelligence Review Agency):** Has declined to review our complaints.
    
3.  **PSIC (Public Sector Integrity Commissioner):** Has declined jurisdiction (Case Ref: PSIC-D-2025-1112), stating they do not review allegations of wrongdoing against specific individuals, even when presented as a group issue.
    
4.  **PCRC (Public Complaints and Review Commission):** This body, intended to provide oversight for CBSA, is not yet operational. _(We can provide written evidence of these refusals upon request.)_
    
As such, **NSICOP is currently the only body with the jurisdiction** to review what we allege to be systemic, subjective inaction by the CBSA.
### The Systemic Issue
The **15 individuals** represented in this complaint share the following characteristics:
*   We are all Chinese citizens admitted to the University of British Columbia (UBC).
    
*   Our study permit applications are all processed by the IRCC Beijing Office.
    
*   We fall under the **VIT 34** category of security screening.
    
*   **Crucially: CSIS has completed its security assessment mandates** and has transmitted its advice to the CBSA.
    
Our files are now stalled at the **Centre for Immigration National Security Screening**, specifically within the "**China and Americas" group** of the CBSA’s Security Screening and Refugee Directorate.
While CSIS has fulfilled its duties, the CBSA has failed to process these files for periods ranging from **six months to over ten months** _after_ receiving the data from CSIS.
### Evidence of Maladministration and Lack of Accountability
We allege that the CBSA is engaged in systemic delays that violate its own operational guidelines and lack transparency:
**1. Violation of Service Standards:** According to the _CBSA Immigration Control (IC) Manual Chapter 1 – Security Screening Process Manual_, our cases fall under _Chapter 10, Screening Guidelines for Temporary Resident Applications_. The stated processing standards are generally within 20 days. Our wait times now exceed **half a year** with zero movement. This suggests a breakdown in the administrative framework governing the IEB.
**2. The "Black Box" of Accountability:** Through Access to Information and Privacy (ATIP) requests, we have confirmed that our status is marked as **“Received by CBSA”** under _ADMISSIBILITIES – SECURITY – SUB ACTIVITIES: 1_. This status has remained unchanged for months.
*   CSIS has done their job.
    
*   IRCC states they are waiting for "Partner Information" (CBSA).
    
*   CBSA refuses to provide updates, directing inquiries back to IRCC. This circular shifting of responsibility creates a lack of accountability where CBSA’s Intelligence and Enforcement Branch effectively operates without a deadline or oversight.
    
**3. Misapplication of National Security Exemptions to Mask Inaction:** CSIS has already completed the primary and most sensitive portion of the security screening in approximately two months. Consequently, the CBSA’s subsequent delay of over six months is inexplicable, given its narrower authority and scope compared to CSIS. Withholding the status of these delayed checks on "national security grounds" is an inappropriate attempt to mask what is essentially **administrative inertia**. The fact that the primary intelligence agency can clear applicants three times faster or more than the CBSA raises serious questions: is this delay a result of **gross inefficiency**, or does it reflect a **subjective, discriminatory practice of intentional inaction against Chinese applicants**?
### Request for Review
We respectfully request that NSICOP, under its mandate to review the **efficacy** of national security and intelligence activities, examine the following:
1.  **The operational efficiency of the CBSA’s [China and Americas] group** within the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening. specifically, regarding the "holding" of files after CSIS assessments are complete.
    
2.  **The systemic delays** facing Chinese Study Permit applicants (specifically VIT 34 cases) and whether these delays constitute a policy of "subjective inaction" rather than legitimate security processing.
    
3.  **The lack of an oversight mechanism** for CBSA security screening delays, given that the PCRC is not active and NSIRA/PSIC have declined jurisdiction.
    
We hope the Committee could investigate why the CBSA is failing to meet its mandate in a timely manner, thereby jeopardizing the education of high-potential students and the reputation of Canada’s immigration system.

**Subject: Systemic Administrative Failure in CBSA Security Screening - Request for Guidance on Complaint Mechanism**

Dear Mr. McGuire,

I am writing to bring to your attention a matter of **systemic inefficiency and lack of accountability within the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB). I submit this report **on behalf of a collective of 15 admitted students** to the University of British Columbia (UBC) to demonstrate that this is not an isolated grievance, but a widespread failure in processing protocols.

### Why We Are Writing to You

We understand that your role as Director General of the International and Border Policy Division involves overseeing the development and implementation of regulations for the newly established **Public Complaints and Review Commission (PCRC)** Act. 

We are writing to you because:

1. **The PCRC is not yet operational**, and therefore we cannot file complaints through the intended independent oversight body for CBSA.

2. **All other review mechanisms have been exhausted or are unavailable:**
   - **CBSA Internal Complaints:** CBSA refused to accept complaints regarding security screening delays.
   - **NSIRA (National Security and Intelligence Review Agency):** Declined to review our complaints.
   - **PSIC (Public Sector Integrity Commissioner):** Declined jurisdiction (Case Ref: PSIC-D-2025-1112), stating they do not review allegations of wrongdoing against specific individuals, even when presented as a group issue.

3. **We have no effective avenue to hold CBSA accountable** for what we believe to be systemic administrative failures.

_(We can provide written evidence of these refusals upon request.)_

### The Systemic Issue

The **15 individuals** represented in this complaint share the following characteristics:

- We are all Chinese citizens admitted to the University of British Columbia (UBC).
- Our study permit applications are all processed by the IRCC Beijing Office.
- We fall under the **VIT 34** category of security screening.
- **Crucially: CSIS has completed its security assessment mandates** and has transmitted its advice to the CBSA.

Our files are now stalled at the **Centre for Immigration National Security Screening**, specifically within the "**China and Americas**" group of the CBSA's Security Screening and Refugee Directorate.

While CSIS has fulfilled its duties, the CBSA has failed to process these files for periods ranging from **six months to over ten months** _after_ receiving the data from CSIS.

### Evidence of Maladministration

**1. Violation of Service Standards:**

According to the _CBSA Immigration Control (IC) Manual Chapter 1 – Security Screening Process Manual_, our cases fall under _Chapter 10, Screening Guidelines for Temporary Resident Applications_. The stated processing standards are generally within **20 days**. 

Our wait times now exceed **six to ten months** with zero movement. This suggests a breakdown in the administrative framework governing the IEB.

**2. The "Black Box" of Accountability:**

Through Access to Information and Privacy (ATIP) requests, we have confirmed that our status is marked as **"Received by CBSA"** under _ADMISSIBILITIES – SECURITY – SUB ACTIVITIES: 1_. This status has remained unchanged for months.

- CSIS has completed their assessment (typically within 2 months).
- IRCC states they are waiting for "Partner Information" (CBSA).
- CBSA refuses to provide updates, directing inquiries back to IRCC.

This circular shifting of responsibility creates a lack of accountability where CBSA's Intelligence and Enforcement Branch effectively operates without oversight.

**3. Disproportionate Delays Compared to CSIS:**

CSIS, which conducts the primary and most sensitive portion of security screening, completes its assessments in approximately **two months**. The CBSA's subsequent delay of over **six months** is inexplicable, given its narrower authority and scope.

This raises serious questions: Is this delay a result of **gross inefficiency**, or does it reflect a **subjective, discriminatory practice of intentional inaction against Chinese applicants**?

### Our Requests

**1. Guidance on Current Complaint Mechanisms:**

Given that the PCRC is not yet operational, **could you please direct us to the appropriate authority** or mechanism where we can file a formal complaint against CBSA for these systemic delays?

**2. Timeline for PCRC Implementation:**

Could you provide an update on the **expected timeline for the PCRC to become operational**? We understand that regulations are currently being developed, and we would appreciate any information on when the Commission will be able to accept and review complaints.

**3. Interim Oversight:**

In the absence of the PCRC, is there any **interim oversight mechanism** through which complainants can hold CBSA accountable for administrative failures in security screening processes?

**4. Policy Review Consideration:**

We respectfully request that Public Safety Canada consider reviewing:
- The operational efficiency of CBSA's China and Americas group within the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening.
- The systemic delays facing Chinese study permit applicants (specifically VIT 34 cases).
- The lack of transparency and accountability in CBSA's post-CSIS security screening process.

**Subject: Formal Complaint Regarding Systemic Administrative Failure and Prolonged Delays in CBSA Security Screening**

Dear Public Safety Canada,

I am writing to file a **formal complaint** on behalf of **15 admitted students** to the University of British Columbia (UBC) regarding **systemic inefficiency, lack of accountability, and what appears to be discriminatory inaction** within the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB).

This complaint demonstrates that these issues are not isolated incidents, but rather a **widespread failure in processing protocols** that warrants urgent investigation and remedial action by Public Safety Canada.

---

### Exhaustion of All Available Review Mechanisms

We are submitting this complaint to Public Safety Canada as the department responsible for overseeing CBSA operations because **all other avenues for accountability have been exhausted or declined jurisdiction:**

1. **CBSA Internal Complaints:** CBSA refused to accept complaints regarding security screening delays, stating they do not address processing times.

2. **NSIRA (National Security and Intelligence Review Agency):** Declined to review our complaints, stating they fall outside their mandate.

3. **PSIC (Public Sector Integrity Commissioner):** Declined jurisdiction (Case Ref: PSIC-D-2025-1112), stating they do not review allegations of wrongdoing against specific individuals, even when presented as a systemic group issue.

4. **PCRC (Public Complaints and Review Commission):** The newly established independent review body for CBSA is **not yet operational**.

_(We can provide written evidence of these refusals upon request.)_

**As Public Safety Canada is the department with direct ministerial oversight of CBSA**, we have no alternative but to bring this systemic failure to your attention and request intervention.

---

### The Systemic Issue: Prolonged, Unexplained Delays

The **15 individuals** represented in this complaint share the following characteristics:

- We are all **Chinese citizens** admitted to the **University of British Columbia (UBC)** for graduate studies or research positions.
- Our study permit applications are processed by the **IRCC Beijing Office**.
- We all fall under the **VIT 34** category requiring security screening.
- **CSIS has completed its security assessment mandates** and transmitted its advice to CBSA.

Despite CSIS completing its assessments (typically within 2 months), our files have been **stalled at CBSA's Centre for Immigration National Security Screening** (specifically the "**China and Americas**" group within the Security Screening and Refugee Directorate) for periods ranging from **six months to over ten months** with no progress.

---

### Evidence of Administrative Failure and Lack of Accountability

We submit the following evidence that CBSA is engaged in systemic delays that violate its own operational guidelines:

#### **1. Violation of CBSA's Own Service Standards**

According to the _CBSA Immigration Control (IC) Manual Chapter 1 – Security Screening Process Manual_, our cases fall under **Chapter 10: Screening Guidelines for Temporary Resident Applications**. 

The stated processing standard is **approximately 20 days**.

Our actual wait times: **180 to 300+ days** (six to ten months) with **zero case movement**.

This represents a failure rate of **900% to 1,500%** beyond CBSA's own stated service standards.

#### **2. The "Black Box" of Unaccountability**

Through **Access to Information and Privacy (ATIP)** requests, we have confirmed that our applications have been marked as **"Received by CBSA"** under the status:

> _ADMISSIBILITIES – SECURITY – SUB ACTIVITIES: 1_

This status has remained **completely unchanged** for 6-10 months.

Meanwhile:
- **CSIS** has completed its security assessments (the most sensitive component) in approximately **2 months**.
- **IRCC** states it is waiting for "Partner Information" from CBSA.
- **CBSA** refuses to provide status updates, directing all inquiries back to IRCC.

This creates a **circular loop of responsibility-shifting** where CBSA effectively operates without deadlines, oversight, or accountability.

#### **3. Disproportionate and Unexplained Delays Compared to CSIS**

The timeline disparity is striking:

| Agency | Processing Time | Nature of Work |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **CSIS** | ~2 months | Primary intelligence assessment (most sensitive) |
| **CBSA** | **6-10+ months** | Administrative review (narrower scope) |

**CSIS**, which conducts the primary and most security-sensitive portion of screening, completes its work in **one-quarter to one-fifth** the time that CBSA takes for what should be a more routine administrative task.

This raises the question: **Is this extreme delay the result of gross administrative inefficiency, or does it reflect a policy of subjective, discriminatory inaction targeting Chinese applicants?**

#### **4. Misuse of "National Security" Exemptions to Mask Inaction**

CBSA has repeatedly invoked "national security" grounds to refuse transparency regarding case status. However:

- The **primary security assessment** (by CSIS) is already complete.
- CBSA's role is **administrative processing** of CSIS recommendations.
- CBSA's refusal to provide even basic procedural updates (e.g., "under review," "awaiting additional information") **cannot be justified on national security grounds** when the intelligence work is already done.

We believe CBSA is **misusing national security exemptions** to conceal what is fundamentally **administrative negligence or discriminatory delay tactics**.

---

### Impact on Individuals and Canada's Reputation

These delays have caused:

1. **Educational Disruption:** Students missing entire academic terms, jeopardizing research projects and degree timelines.

2. **Financial Harm:** Ongoing costs for tuition deposits, accommodation, and living expenses without the ability to commence studies.

3. **Psychological Distress:** Uncertainty, anxiety, and the feeling of being subjected to discriminatory treatment.

4. **Damage to Canada's Reputation:** Undermining Canada's image as a welcoming destination for international students and researchers.

5. **Loss of Talent:** High-potential graduate students in STEM fields considering alternative destinations (US, UK, Australia) due to Canada's broken immigration processing.

---

### Specific Requests to Public Safety Canada

We respectfully request that Public Safety Canada, as the department responsible for CBSA oversight:

#### **1. Investigate CBSA's Processing Delays**

Conduct an investigation into:
- Why the "China and Americas" group within CBSA's Centre for Immigration National Security Screening is experiencing processing times **900-1500% beyond stated service standards**.
- Whether these delays constitute **discriminatory treatment** of Chinese applicants.
- Whether CBSA has adequate staffing, resources, or operational procedures to fulfill its mandate.

#### **2. Order Immediate Case Reviews**

Direct CBSA to:
- Conduct immediate reviews of the 15 cases listed below.
- Provide substantive status updates to applicants within **30 days**.
- Establish a timeline for resolution of cases where CSIS assessments are already complete.

#### **3. Establish Accountability Mechanisms**

Require CBSA to:
- Publish processing time statistics for security screening (broken down by country/region and screening category).
- Implement a transparent case status system that provides applicants with meaningful updates.
- Establish penalties or consequences for systematic failure to meet service standards.

#### **4. Review CBSA's Use of "National Security" Exemptions**

Examine whether CBSA is inappropriately using national security exemptions to avoid accountability for administrative delays, particularly in cases where CSIS has already completed its assessment.

#### **5. Interim Reporting**

Provide us with:
- An acknowledgment of receipt of this complaint within **15 business days**.
- A preliminary response outlining what steps Public Safety Canada will take within **30 days**.
- A substantive response and action plan within **60 days**.

# 投诉信给Canadian Human Rights Commission

---

**Subject: Formal Complaint of Systemic Discrimination by CBSA Against Chinese Applicants in Security Screening Process**

Dear Canadian Human Rights Commission,

I am writing to file a **formal complaint under the _Canadian Human Rights Act_** on behalf of **15 Chinese students** admitted to the University of British Columbia (UBC), alleging **systemic discrimination based on national or ethnic origin** by the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)** in its security screening process.

We believe that CBSA's Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB) is engaging in a pattern of **discriminatory delays and inaction** that disproportionately targets Chinese applicants, resulting in prolonged processing times that far exceed those experienced by applicants from other countries and that violate CBSA's own service standards.

---

### Summary of Discrimination Complaint

**Ground of Discrimination:** National or ethnic origin (Chinese citizenship)

**Service Provider:** Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)

**Nature of Discrimination:** Systemic delays and refusal to process study permit applications in a timely manner, applied disproportionately to Chinese applicants

**Pattern of Discriminatory Treatment:**
- Processing times for Chinese VIT 34 applicants: **6-10+ months** after CSIS completion
- CBSA's stated service standard: **20 days**
- Actual delay: **900-1500% beyond service standards**
- No comparable delays reported for applicants from other countries in similar security screening categories

---

### The Discriminatory Pattern: A Systemic Failure Targeting Chinese Applicants

#### **1. The 15 Affected Individuals**

All 15 complainants share the following characteristics:

- **National origin:** All are Chinese citizens
- **Academic merit:** All admitted to graduate programs at UBC (a top-tier Canadian university)
- **Security category:** All fall under VIT 34 screening
- **CSIS status:** All have **completed CSIS security assessments**
- **Processing office:** All processed through IRCC Beijing Office
- **Current status:** All stalled at CBSA's "**China and Americas**" group within the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening

Despite CSIS completing its assessments (typically within 2 months), CBSA has failed to process our files for **6 to 10+ months**, with an average delay of **237 days** (nearly 8 months) since CSIS transmitted its recommendations to CBSA.

#### **2. Evidence of Disparate Treatment**

| Metric | Chinese VIT 34 Applicants (Our Cases) | CBSA Service Standard | Disparity |
|--------|----------------------------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| **CBSA Processing Time** | 180-340+ days | 20 days | **900-1700%** longer |
| **Average Delay** | 237 days | 20 days | **1185%** longer |
| **Longest Case** | 340 days | 20 days | **1700%** longer |

While we lack comprehensive comparative data (due to CBSA's lack of transparency), anecdotal evidence from international student communities suggests:

- Applicants from **Western countries** in comparable security screening categories experience delays measured in **weeks, not months**
- Applicants from **non-Chinese Asian countries** report significantly shorter processing times even in VIT 34 category
- Chinese applicants consistently report the longest delays and highest rates of "administrative processing" limbo

#### **3. The "China and Americas" Group: A Structural Indicator of Discriminatory Processing**

Our files are specifically held by CBSA's "**China and Americas**" group—a designation that suggests country-based processing streams.

The existence of a China-specific processing unit, combined with:
- Dramatically longer processing times
- Complete lack of transparency
- Refusal to provide status updates
- No accountability mechanisms

...strongly suggests that CBSA has implemented a **de facto policy of discriminatory delay** against Chinese applicants.

#### **4. Circular Accountability Void and Its Discriminatory Impact**

CBSA's refusal to accept accountability has a disproportionate impact on Chinese applicants:

- **CSIS** completes assessments in ~2 months (for all nationalities)
- **CBSA** delays Chinese cases for 6-10+ months after receiving CSIS advice
- **IRCC** states it is waiting for CBSA
- **CBSA** refuses to provide updates, directing inquiries to IRCC
- **Result:** Chinese applicants trapped in an accountability void with no recourse

When combined with the statistical evidence of dramatically longer processing times specifically for Chinese applicants, this pattern constitutes **systemic discrimination**.

---

### Exhaustion of Alternative Remedies

We have attempted to address this issue through every available mechanism:

1. **CBSA Internal Complaints:** Refused to accept complaints about processing times.

2. **NSIRA (National Security and Intelligence Review Agency):** We filed complaints with NSIRA regarding CBSA's conduct. **NSIRA declined**, stating they primarily review CSIS activities and that public complaints alone are insufficient to trigger a review of CBSA operations, even though NSIRA has statutory authority to review CBSA under its enabling legislation.

3. **PSIC (Public Sector Integrity Commissioner):** Declined jurisdiction (Case Ref: PSIC-D-2025-1112).

4. **PCRC (Public Complaints and Review Commission):** Not yet operational.

_(Written evidence of these refusals available upon request.)_

**As a result, the Canadian Human Rights Commission is our only remaining avenue** to challenge what we believe is systemic discrimination by a federal service provider.

---

### Legal Basis for Discrimination Complaint

#### **Canadian Human Rights Act, Section 5:**

> "It is a discriminatory practice in the provision of goods, services, facilities or accommodation customarily available to the general public [...] to differentiate adversely in relation to that individual on a prohibited ground of discrimination."

**Analysis:**
- **Service:** Study permit processing (immigration services)
- **Service provider:** CBSA (federal government agency)
- **Prohibited ground:** National or ethnic origin (Chinese citizenship)
- **Adverse differentiation:** Processing times 900-1700% longer than stated standards, disproportionately affecting Chinese applicants

#### **Pattern of Systemic Discrimination:**

The discrimination is not based on individual security concerns (which would be handled by CSIS and already completed in our cases), but rather on:

1. **Administrative processing structures** (country-specific "China and Americas" unit)
2. **Blanket application of extreme delays** to all Chinese applicants regardless of individual circumstances
3. **Lack of justification** for delays (CSIS already completed security work)
4. **Disparate impact** compared to applicants from other countries

This pattern meets the definition of **systemic discrimination**: discrimination that is embedded in policies, practices, or organizational structures that result in adverse treatment of members of a particular group.

---

### Precedent: NSIRA Investigation into Immigration Processing

We note that there is **significant precedent** for human rights complaints triggering national security agency reviews in immigration contexts.

The **NSIRA investigation** documented here:

**[Allegations against CSIS, IRCC, CBSA and Public Safety Canada in Relation to Their Role in Processing Immigration Applications](https://nsira-ossnr.gc.ca/en/investigations/investigation-summaries/allegations-against-csis-immigration-refugees-and-citizenship-canada-the-canada-border-services-agency-and-public-safety-canada-in-relation-to-their-role-in-processing-immigration-applications/)**

...was initiated following complaints received by the Canadian Human Rights Commission and subsequently referred to NSIRA.

#### Key Parallels to Our Case:

From the NSIRA investigation summary:

> "NSIRA received two complaints **alleging that certain government departments were discriminating against immigration applicants based on their national origin.** [...]
>
> The complainants alleged that **IRCC, CBSA, and PS were unfairly delaying the processing of certain immigration applications** based on the applicant's national or ethnic origin, and that **CSIS was contributing to those delays.**"

**This mirrors our situation exactly:**
- Allegations of discrimination based on national origin ✓
- Allegations of unfair delays in immigration processing ✓
- Involvement of CBSA in discriminatory practices ✓
- CSIS role (though in our case, CSIS has actually completed its work) ✓

#### The Outcome of That Case:

NSIRA's investigation found that while there were legitimate security concerns in some cases, there were also **procedural delays and lack of transparency** that disproportionately affected certain applicants.

**Critically, the investigation was only possible because the Canadian Human Rights Commission referred the matter to NSIRA.**

---

### Request for CHRC Action and NSIRA Referral

Given the precedent established in the case cited above, we respectfully request that the Canadian Human Rights Commission:

#### **1. Accept This Complaint for Investigation**

We request that CHRC:
- Accept this complaint as prima facie evidence of systemic discrimination by CBSA
- Open a formal investigation into CBSA's processing of Chinese VIT 34 study permit applications
- Examine whether CBSA's "China and Americas" processing unit operates with discriminatory intent or effect

#### **2. Invoke Subsection 45(2) of the Canadian Human Rights Act**

**Section 45(2) states:**

> "The Commission may at any stage **refer a complaint to the National Security and Intelligence Review Agency** for investigation and report if, in the opinion of the Commission, the complaint **relates to a discriminatory practice described in section 5 or 7** that is **alleged to have been committed by a member or employee of CSIS, CBSA, CSE or a department or agency set out in Schedule 3.**"

**We request that CHRC exercise this authority** to refer our complaint to NSIRA for investigation and report, specifically regarding:

- Whether CBSA's Intelligence and Enforcement Branch is engaging in discriminatory processing of Chinese study permit applications
- Whether the "China and Americas" unit within CBSA's Centre for Immigration National Security Screening operates with procedures that constitute systemic discrimination
- Whether CBSA's refusal to process cases in a timely manner after CSIS completion is justified by legitimate security concerns or constitutes discriminatory delay

#### **3. Why NSIRA Referral Is Necessary**

As noted above, **we have already attempted to file complaints with NSIRA directly, and NSIRA declined**, stating:

> "NSIRA primarily reviews CSIS activities. While we have statutory authority to review CBSA, public complaints alone are insufficient to trigger a review of CBSA operations. We recommend directing your concerns to the appropriate oversight body."

However, **under subsection 45(2) of the Canadian Human Rights Act**, a **referral from the Canadian Human Rights Commission carries different weight** than individual public complaints.

The precedent case (linked above) demonstrates that:
- CHRC can refer discrimination complaints to NSIRA
- NSIRA will investigate CBSA when the complaint is referred by CHRC
- Such investigations can result in findings and recommendations for systemic change

**We believe that CHRC referral is the only mechanism** that will compel NSIRA to examine CBSA's discriminatory practices in our cases.
